text: I'm going to lose (or have already lost, I guess) a lot of the Flash art and games and interactive toys I created while learning programming through the 00s. Sucks that it has to be that way, but I have such fond memories, and I'm glad that I got to grow up in that scene (so to speak).

elements.text: I'm going to lose (or have already lost, I guess) a lot of the Flash art and games and interactive toys I created while learning programming through the 00s. Sucks that it has to be that way, but I have such fond memories, and I'm glad that I got to grow up in that scene (so to speak).

text: Yes, I think there are undeniably things this can't do!

elements.text: Yes, I think there are undeniably things this can't do!

text: And we've really only just scratched the surface of the problem domain at this point.

elements.text: And we've really only just scratched the surface of the problem domain at this point.

text: My collaborator, Orion Henry, had a vision of an overarching schema that covered all your data as one unified type, which means that augmentation would usually just be another case of relocation.

elements.text: My collaborator, Orion Henry, had a vision of an overarching schema that covered all your data as one unified type, which means that augmentation would usually just be another case of relocation.

text: In general, I observe that we try to maintain backwards compatibility, so in general, we're already doing these things in an ad hoc way.

elements.text: In general, I observe that we try to maintain backwards compatibility, so in general, we're already doing these things in an ad hoc way.

text: The other thing I hope this work can do is basically make all the easy moves easy, and make it a conscious decision when and how to do things that are irreversible, instead of realizing later when you can't do a `rails db:down` or whatever ActiveRecord calls it.

elements.text: The other thing I hope this work can do is basically make all the easy moves easy, and make it a conscious decision when and how to do things that are irreversible, instead of realizing later when you can't do a
rails db:down
or whatever ActiveRecord calls it.

text: All that said, I hope we'll manage to provoke you or someone else to think about how to extend this basic model to solve the real problems you have.

elements.text: All that said, I hope we'll manage to provoke you or someone else to think about how to extend this basic model to solve the real problems you have.

text: Has anyone seen any paradigms they really like for keyboard-navigating big, abstract mediums that aren't text? i really like how text can be keyboard navigated easily. Where your cursor + attention is about to go is always clear and we've invented lots of ways to accelerate getting your cursor to where you want it to go. How does that play out in things that aren't text though, like all the flow based languages we're seeing, or tools like Webflow that are trying to make it fast to create trees of stuff? Are we destined for slower mouse based interfaces that don't leverage muscle memory as much?

elements.text: Has anyone seen any paradigms they really like for keyboard-navigating big, abstract mediums that aren't text? i really like how text can be keyboard navigated easily. Are we destined for slower mouse based interfaces that don't leverage muscle memory as much?

text: i think part of why code feels nice to navigate is that it matches the paper metaphor that we're all very used to, things don't jump around or appear or stack on top of each other when you're working in a (normal) text editor, it's 2D, which monitors and the arrow keys are also

elements.text: i think part of why code feels nice to navigate is that it matches the paper metaphor that we're all very used to, things don't jump around or appear or stack on top of each other when you're working in a (normal) text editor, it's 2D, which monitors and the arrow keys are also

text: one thing i've been exploring is TV-remote style navigation of interfaces where the interface knows what all the selectable targets are and the arrow keys move a cursor in space between all the targets, like the Apple TV or Roku UI, but it suffers from some issues where it's unclear where your cursor might go to next, and that compounds as you jump farther so it's hard to make accelerated key commands like jump all the way to left or right or something of that nature.

elements.text: one thing i've been exploring is TV-remote style navigation of interfaces where the interface knows what all the selectable targets are and the arrow keys move a cursor in space between all the targets, like the Apple TV or Roku UI, but it suffers from some issues where it's unclear where your cursor might go to next, and that compounds as you jump farther so it's hard to make accelerated key commands like jump all the way to left or right or something of that nature.

text: text files also don't have inner hidden bits that you have to open by clicking something or buried menus, the only way you can change which part of a text file you are seeing is by scrolling, which again matches the paper metaphor really closely. we've invented all sorts of fun ways to jump around the different text files to allow developers to go in and out of abstractions and levels of detail, but the artifacts that express that detail don't really compress themselves or expand or change shape, unless you're using code folding, which i always found cumbersome

text: <@UFXFB1RK8> I was wondering if you guys had a look at Pijul, which seems to do the same thing albeit in the version control setting with their Patch theory: <https://pijul.org/model/#why-care-about-patch-theory>

attachments.title: Pijul
attachments.text: An easy to use, distributed and fast version control system.

elements.text: I was wondering if you guys had a look at Pijul, which seems to do the same thing albeit in the version control setting with their Patch theory:

text: As I understand it, Pijul is closer to automerge / CRDT theory than to our work on lenses.

elements.text: As I understand it, Pijul is closer to automerge / CRDT theory than to our work on lenses.

text: To expand, the Pijul's program's type is a folder of files with their pushouts.

elements.text: To expand, the Pijul's program's type is a folder of files with their pushouts.

text: (Using their terminology here.)

elements.text: (Using their terminology here.)

text: Pijul doesn't have to contend with changing definitions of what a folder or a file are. The semantics are basically "POSIX but with, you know, some weird quirks".

elements.text: Pijul doesn't have to contend with changing definitions of what a folder or a file are. The semantics are basically "POSIX but with, you know, some weird quirks".

text: Automerge's position is (roughly) that "operating over a sequence of bytes is insufficiently expressive to model change over time"

elements.text: Automerge's position is (roughly) that "operating over a sequence of bytes is insufficiently expressive to model change over time"

text: So now you have these structured documents you have to figure out. Changes to them will always merge cleanly into a new valid document, which is great. You never get structure-destroying git style merge conflicts

elements.text: So now you have these structured documents you have to figure out. Changes to them will always merge cleanly into a new valid document, which is great. You never get structure-destroying git style merge conflicts

text: but automerge doesn't tell you what the structure of a document SHOULD BE

elements.text: but automerge doesn't tell you what the structure of a document SHOULD BE

text: so if your document expects to find the title in /head/title but someone else has put it into /metadata/title, you're gonna crash when you open that

elements.text: so if your document expects to find the title in /head/title but someone else has put it into /metadata/title, you're gonna crash when you open that

text: to make matters worse, our local-first work with realtime collaboration means that if you want to move from /head/title to /metadata/title you're gonna have a real bad time when other folks are still out there expecting the former

elements.text: to make matters worse, our local-first work with realtime collaboration means that if you want to move from /head/title to /metadata/title you're gonna have a real bad time when other folks are still out there expecting the former

text: the traditional solution here is a strictly defined schema and a protocol version, which is problematic, because i might upgrade and then get a bunch of writes from your old version which i would then have to throw away

elements.text: the traditional solution here is a strictly defined schema and a protocol version, which is problematic, because i might upgrade and then get a bunch of writes from your old version which i would then have to throw away

text: the "cambrian era" argument is that not only can linearly different versions of software interoperate, *diverging versions* can interoperate within certain bounds

elements.text: the "cambrian era" argument is that not only can linearly different versions of software interoperate,
diverging versions
can interoperate within certain bounds

text:
<https://www.theverge.com/2017/10/19/16503076/oracle-vs-google-judge-william-alsup-interview-waymo-uber|https://www.theverge.com/2017/10/19/16503076/oracle-vs-google-judge-william-alsup-interview-waymo-uber> this is an absolutely wild article

> The judge sat me down on the sofa and walked me through his programs on a 2011 court-issued Dell laptop. He couldn’t run the same programs on his desktop computer, he said with some irritation, so the Dell was here to stay. “It’s the last one that will support QuickBASIC, which is kind of a shame, because it’s the only language I really know.”
> 
...

> I asked him if I could put his code on GitHub, and he asked me what GitHub was. In lieu of that, he handed me printouts of his computer programs, three stacks of paper that had been neatly stapled at the corners. The one on the top, he apologized, had several dependencies that he hadn’t had the time to print out. Long before he became the judge presiding over Silicon Valley, Alsup had been a hobbyist operating in isolation; he’s a geek, but he’s a geek from another era.

attachments.title: How the judge on Oracle v. Google taught himself to code
attachments.text: Judge William Alsup taught himself to code for fun in the 1980s. Now he's the judge on Silicon Valley's biggest cases.
attachments.fallback: The Verge: How the judge on Oracle v. Google taught himself to code

elements.text: https://www.theverge.com/2017/10/19/16503076/oracle-vs-google-judge-william-alsup-interview-waymo-uber
this is an absolutely wild article

The judge sat me down on the sofa and walked me through his programs on a 2011 court-issued Dell laptop. He couldn’t run the same programs on his desktop computer, he said with some irritation, so the Dell was here to stay. “It’s the last one that will support QuickBASIC, which is kind of a shame, because it’s the only language I really know.”

I asked him if I could put his code on GitHub, and he asked me what GitHub was. In lieu of that, he handed me printouts of his computer programs, three stacks of paper that had been neatly stapled at the corners. The one on the top, he apologized, had several dependencies that he hadn’t had the time to print out. Long before he became the judge presiding over Silicon Valley, Alsup had been a hobbyist operating in isolation; he’s a geek, but he’s a geek from another era.

text:
I feel like this is the perfect guy for the job. He understands all the core technical concepts without being bogged down by the trends, hype, and influence of modern tech society. This allows him to be impartial without being ignorant.

The last thing I want is a Judge who spends too much time on Hacker News or Reddit...

text: Alas, it is not he who is deciding the case. He already did, but then it went to a higher court.

text: Indeed, The Supreme Court has agreed to hear the case this time around. It's going to be a landmark case either way: <https://www.npr.org/2020/10/07/921018204/google-and-oracles-decade-long-copyright-battle-reaches-supreme-court>

attachments.title: Google And Oracle's Decade-Long Copyright Battle Reaches Supreme Court
attachments.text: Oracle accuses Google of illegally copying its software. Google contends the kind of code it used cannot be owned by anyone.
attachments.fallback: NPR.org: Google And Oracle's Decade-Long Copyright Battle Reaches Supreme Court
